title: Fix arch_generate JSON+min_degree filter returning empty graph
description: When arch_generate is called with format=json and min_degree>0, the result is {nodes:[], edges:[]} even when the underlying graph has nodes meeting the degree threshold. The forcegraph format applies min_degree correctly. Reproduced today on AI-Knowledge vault scan (292 nodes, 1282 edges) — JSON output empty, forcegraph output correctly produced 290KB filtered file. Root cause likely missing filter call in internal/render/json.go. Bug found by dogfooding sift workflow on the user's own MCP server.
close_reason: Misdiagnosis. Re-tested with min_degree=8 on same vault: JSON renderer correctly returned 16 nodes / ~85 edges. The min_degree=10 empty result was correct iterative-cascade behavior — KeepHighDegree iterates until stable, and on a sparse 292-node vault with hub-and-spoke topology, min_degree=10 cascades to zero (leaves drop, hubs lose connections, hubs drop). Code is correct; UX is poor. Filing replacement bead for the actual UX issue.

title: Warn when min_degree filter cascades to empty graph
description: When arch_generate is called with min_degree above what a sparse graph can support, the iterative KeepHighDegree filter cascades to zero nodes. Output is silently empty {nodes:[], edges:[]} with no indication of why. Surfaced today during dogfood test on AI-Knowledge vault: min_degree=10 returned empty, min_degree=8 returned 16 nodes. Fix: detect cascade-to-empty in PrepareGraph (input had nodes, output has zero), surface a warning in the renderer response or summary that suggests trying a lower threshold. Minimal scope: add warning string to response; renderers attach it to summary. Found via sift dogfood test 26-04-2026.
close_reason: Fixed in same session as filing. Added Notes []string field to ArchGenerateResult and a warning in archGenerate handler when min_degree>0 cascades the graph to zero nodes. Added two regression tests in internal/render/common_test.go documenting the cascade behavior (CascadeToEmpty + KeepsDenseSubgraph). make check + golangci-lint clean.

title: Add 'Why not X' comparative section to README
description: Lift content from ~/Documents/obsidian-home/AI-Knowledge/CodeFlow vs code-to-arch-mcp Evaluation 2026-04-22.md into README. Section sits before Tools, after What you get. Cover: codeflow (browser, human-facing, file-granular), CodeGraphContext (Python, graph-DB-backed), graphify (skill-scope, multimodal). Honest framing: 'this tool wins on agent-facing MCP transport + drift narrative + cross-substrate. It loses on browser-native UI and stars.' Decision-makers want this BEFORE installing.

title: Record demo GIF showing arch_scan + arch_generate + arch_drift_explain
description: README has had <!-- TODO: Add demo GIF --> marker since launch. Now have real receipts: arch_drift_explain HEAD~10..HEAD on this repo produced a paste-ready PR paragraph; arch_scan on a 292-note Obsidian vault completed in 43ms and surfaced 67 orphans. Use those as the demo material. Capture: arch_scan miro-mcp-server + arch_generate forcegraph + arch_drift_explain on a real PR. Output to /tmp via asciinema or QuickTime, optimize with gifski. Embed in README right after the Why section.

title: Add multi-path scan support: paths []string instead of path string
description: Currently arch_scan accepts a single path. The cross-substrate value proposition (scan code + skills + vault and produce one unified graph) requires multi-path support. Approach: add 'paths' field alongside 'path' (back-compat); merged graph tags each node with source-substrate metadata. Enables 'scan claude-code-config/skills + code-to-arch-mcp/internal + AI-Knowledge' to surface cross-references nobody else can produce. Discovered as gap in dogfood test 26-04-2026.

title: Adoption trial + Merkle DAG compare: zilliztech/claude-context
description:
Two threads on zilliztech/claude-context (8.4k stars, TypeScript MCP server for semantic code search, uses Merkle DAG for incremental indexing):

Thread A — Adoption trial (what-to-steal #2):
1. Install via `claude mcp add claude-context -- npx @zilliz/claude-context-mcp@latest` with Zilliz Cloud + OpenAI embeddings
2. Index 1-2 real codebases (candidates: core-bl for lmwtfy work, or public360-mcp-server itself)
3. Use for a week across actual tasks
4. Note: retrieval quality, latency, cost (Zilliz + OpenAI embeddings), failure modes
5. Decide: keep / adopt / drop

Thread B — Merkle DAG reference reading for code-to-arch-mcp:
1. Read their Merkle DAG indexing implementation (pnpm-workspace monorepo, packages/)
2. Compare against current incremental scan state in internal/infra/persist.go
3. Document differences in approach (hashing strategy, subtree detection, change propagation)
4. Decide whether to port any patterns; commit #294 today removed unreachable modified-detection, so the implementation is actively being sharpened

Source: https://github.com/zilliztech/claude-context
Sibling: https://github.com/zilliztech/memsearch (persistent memory MCP plugin)
Background from 23-04-2026 sift: [[Building Agents That Reach Production Systems With MCP - Anthropic]]
close_reason: Thread B (Merkle DAG comparison doc) shipped in PR #4 / c6f467e. Thread A (week-long adoption trial) split out as a separate bead.

title: Add HTML output format (Option D: Mermaid-embed self-contained)
description:
Add HTML output format to `arch_generate` for human-facing codebase review. Produces a self-contained HTML file with embedded Mermaid diagram.

Scope (Option D from the CodeFlow evaluation):
- ~100 lines of new Go code
- ~600KB output file (self-contained via `go:embed`)
- Reuses the existing Mermaid renderer; no new layout logic to maintain
- Candidate API: `arch_generate --format html` or a new `arch_generate_html` tool

Defaults:
- View level: `component` (the `container` level returns empty output for Go MCP servers because they have packages and endpoints, not service-type nodes; document this)

Why Option D (Mermaid-embed) and not Option C (D3 force graph):
- Mermaid is sufficient for repos up to ~50 nodes; reference repos tested (miro-mcp-server, mediawiki-mcp-server, code-to-arch-mcp) are 15-17 nodes
- D3 path is 4-5 days vs 1 day for Mermaid-embed
- Can upgrade to D3 later if users need it for larger repos

Acceptance:
- `arch_generate` supports `format=html`
- Output file is self-contained (no network dependencies when opened in browser)
- Default view level is `component`
- Tool description documents the Go-MCP-servers-container-is-empty behavior
- Tests cover render output structure

Reference: `/tmp/codeflow-vs-code-to-arch-eval-2026-04-22.md`
Parent bead: code-to-arch-mcp-97x
close_reason: HTML output format (Option D, Mermaid-embed) shipped in PR #5 / 480716b. Self-contained ~900 KB output, defaults to component view for Go MCP servers.

title: Evaluate CodeFlow vs code-to-arch-mcp on 3 reference repos; decide on HTML-output format feature
description:
Paste: https://github.com/braedonsaunders/codeflow

Plan:
1. Run CodeFlow (hosted at codeflow-five.vercel.app) on 3 repos: miro-mcp-server, public360-mcp-server, novuhq/novu.
2. Run code-to-arch-mcp (arch_generate with Mermaid, PlantUML, C4) on same 3 repos.
3. Compare: what does each surface that the other misses?
4. Decide: should code-to-arch-mcp add an HTML-output format (self-contained D3 viewer) as a tool? Candidate API: arch_generate_html or --format html.
5. If yes: scope the feature as a follow-up bead.

Context:
- CodeFlow = single index.html, D3-based, browser-only, privacy-first
- code-to-arch-mcp = Go MCP server, agent-facing output
- Different consumers: humans vs agents. HTML output would serve humans (PR reviewers, stakeholders)
- Reference sift: AI-Knowledge/Sift Reading Log.md entry 21-04-2026

Estimated: 2-3 hours.
close_reason: Decision baked into shipped child 97x.1 (Option D Mermaid-embed). Eval purpose served; HTML format is now in main.

title: Submit code-to-arch-mcp to anthropics/knowledge-work-plugins marketplace
description: Anthropic shipped knowledge-work-plugins marketplace 23-04-2026. Eight Brilliant-rated sifts in the reading log point at marketplace governance / Cat 24 / SkillCheck positioning. Own MCP server isn't listed yet. Eat-own-dogfood signal. Blockers/dependencies: demo GIF (task above), README comparative section (task above), benchmark numbers (task above). Once those three land, submit via PR to anthropics/knowledge-work-plugins. Expected response time: probably 1-2 weeks based on early-stage marketplace patterns.

title: Add benchmark file to verify 'milliseconds' incremental scan claim
description: README claims first scan a few seconds, follow-up milliseconds. Currently unverifiable. Create tests/benchmark_test.go using Go's testing.B against fixed targets: ~/Projects/miro-mcp-server (large), ~/Projects/gleif-mcp-server (medium), ~/Projects/code-to-arch-mcp (self). Emit wall-clock numbers for cold + warm cache. Convert marketing claim into falsifiable receipt; output usable as evidence in README + Substack post + plugin marketplace submission.

title: Add arch_drift_explain — natural-language summary of structural diff
description:
arch_drift returns a structured diff. The 'share via PR description / standup channel' use case wants 3-5 sentences in the user's voice: 'Between v1.2 and main, you added a Postgres dependency to internal/billing (new), removed the rate-limiter package, and the auth service now talks to Redis where before it didn't.'

Drift IS the killer feature of this tool; arch_drift_explain is the narrative wrapper that makes the output shareable. Today the consumer has to read a JSON diff and write the summary themselves.

Implementation sketch:
- New tool arch_drift_explain (or a 'narrative=true' arg on arch_drift)
- Walks the structured diff and groups changes by node type and severity
- Emits a templated paragraph: 'Between {ref_a} and {ref_b}, ... [N nodes added: ...]. [M nodes removed: ...]. [K dependency edges changed: ...]. The biggest structural change is {largest delta}.'
- No LLM call — pure templating. The MCP caller will paraphrase if they want.

Acceptance:
1. Tool emits a paragraph for any non-empty drift
2. 'no drift' input emits a single sentence ('No structural change between X and Y.')
3. Test against this repo's own diff between two recent tags
4. Pair with arch_drift in the README's killer-feature section
close_reason: Implemented arch_drift_explain. internal/drift/narrative.go has the deterministic templating function (no LLM); tools/ wires it as the 19th MCP tool and delegates to archDrift+Narrate. 10 unit tests cover empty diff, nil report, added/removed/modified nodes, edges-only, circular-dep validation, list truncation, type grouping, sentence count. README updated: tool count 18->19, killer-feature section, both tool tables. make check passes.

title: Explore: cross-substrate composition (code + notes + configs in one graph)
description:
**Status: exploration, not committed implementation.** The conceptual move that would push this tool from Good to Brilliant is treating code, markdown notes, infra configs, OKR docs, etc. as graph substrates of equal weight — and supporting cross-substrate queries: which design-decision notes are referenced nowhere in code? Which packages implement no documented decision? Which OKR has no commits flowing into it?

The substrate-detection layer already exists (scanner.New routes by file extension). What's missing is:
- A way to mark certain edges as cross-substrate (e.g., a note referencing a Go package by name should produce an edge from Note→Package, not just Note→Note)
- A new tool primitive (arch_orphan_decisions or similar) that walks edges across substrate boundaries to find disconnects
- A naming/framing for the move — 'architecture-as-substrate-graph' is currently nameless in the field

Open questions:
1. How does a markdown note reference a Go package? Inline code spans? An explicit annotation syntax? File path matching?
2. What's the right unit on the OKR / decision-doc side — frontmatter tags? A YAML manifest?
3. Does this stay one MCP server or split out (each substrate-bridge as its own analyzer)?

Spike scope: 4 hours. Pick one cross-substrate edge type (notes → Go packages by name match), implement, test on this repo's own README ↔ internal/ tree.
close_reason: Spike implemented: LinkNotesToPackages in internal/detector/notes_to_packages.go matches inline-code-span path tokens against package directory suffixes (confidence 0.6). Tests cover unit (normalize/path-suffix/dedupe) plus integration on this repo's README ↔ internal/ tree, which produced 15 real edges (CLAUDE.md → 11 packages, design notes → 4). Findings written up in docs/cross-substrate-exploration.md: code-span matching is sufficient for design-doc use case, plain prose is too noisy, README produces zero edges (uses prose not code spans), recommended graduation path documented. Not wired into any MCP tool — graduation decision is the next bead to file.

title: Add go install one-liner to README setup
description: Current Setup section assumes 'git clone && make build', which is high-friction for try-before-adopt. A 'go install github.com/olgasafonova/code-to-arch-mcp/cmd/code-to-arch@latest' line at the top of Setup cuts the install path to one command. Requires the module path in go.mod to match the GitHub URL, the cmd/code-to-arch package to build cleanly with no replace directives, and (ideally) a tagged release so @latest resolves to a stable point. Verify GOBIN works correctly before promising it in docs.
notes: BLOCKED 2026-04-26: Verified go install ...@latest fails because repo is PRIVATE. Module path matches go.mod, but Go module proxy / GitHub require auth to resolve. Decisions needed before this bead can ship: (1) make repo public, (2) tag a release (e.g. v0.1.0) so @latest is stable. Until then, README should keep the git-clone+make instructions. Re-open after visibility decision.
close_reason: Repo flipped to PUBLIC 2026-04-26, verified 'go install github.com/olgasafonova/code-to-arch-mcp/cmd/code-to-arch@latest' downloads and installs (29MB binary in ~/go/bin/). README Setup section now leads with the one-liner; build-from-source instructions kept as fallback. No tag yet, so @latest resolves to most recent commit on main (currently 5d1e6bf); a v0.1.0 tag is an optional future improvement for stable resolution.

title: Add demo GIF to README
description: The README has a TODO comment at line 17 reserving space for a demo GIF. The single biggest adoption-friction blocker right now is the 'what does this even do' question — a 30-second screencap of arch_scan + arch_generate forcegraph on a real repo + a real vault side-by-side closes that gap. Concrete > abstract feature claims. Suggested capture: terminal split-screen showing the MCP tool call on the left, the rendered HTML/forcegraph on the right, with a representative repo (e.g., a public Go project) and a representative markdown directory (e.g., docs/ of an OSS project). Save as docs/demo.gif, embed in README replacing the TODO.
notes: BLOCKED 2026-04-26 pending manual screen recording. Wrote docs/demo-recording.md with: (1) what to capture (terminal+browser side-by-side, ~30s), (2) suggested target repo + vault, (3) ffmpeg single-step mp4->gif command at 12fps/1200px, (4) gifsicle compression step, (5) target <5MB. Re-open + close after recording exists at docs/demo.gif and TODO at README:17 is replaced with ![demo](docs/demo.gif). The recording is fundamentally a manual artifact — terminal-only recorders (vhs, asciinema) can't show the browser side, which is the point of the demo.

title: Extend arch_scan to markdown substrate (Obsidian wiki-links + relative md links)
description:
Split from 0nz. The substrate-agnostic half. Currently arch_scan parses Go imports; extend to parse markdown files and emit nodes/edges from link relationships.

Tool extension scope:
- Detect substrate by file extension at scan time: .go (existing), .md/.markdown (new)
- Markdown analyzer in internal/analyzer/markdown/ (mirror golang/, typescript/, python/ pattern)
- Parse two link types per file:
  1. Wiki-links: `[[note]]` or `[[note|display]]` (Obsidian convention)
  2. Relative markdown links: `[text](./relative.md)` and `[text](folder/note.md)`
- Output: same Node/Edge shape as Go scan (NodePackage analog: NodeNote? or treat each file as NodePackage)
- Skip code blocks (don't parse links inside fenced code)

Strategic question worth deciding before coding:
- Add NodeNote type, or reuse NodePackage with substrate metadata?
- Single arch_scan that auto-detects, or new arch_scan_markdown tool?

Acceptance:
1. Markdown analyzer with wiki-link + relative-link edge detection
2. Integration test against own AI-Knowledge directory (~150 notes; expect to find orphans + hubs)
3. Tool description updated per existing definitions.go convention
4. golangci-lint clean

Parent: code-to-arch-mcp-0nz (split for scope)
Architectural read: substrate-detection layer + graph algorithm layer (codeflow's approach)
close_reason: Implemented in feat/arch-scan-markdown. Markdown analyzer with NodeNote type, wiki-link + relative .md link extraction, code-block stripping, wikilink resolver in ResolvedEdges. Integration test on AI-Knowledge: 292 notes, 1282 raw / 905 resolved edges (71% rate), 63 hubs, 40 orphans, 44ms scan.

title: Add arch_blast_radius tool: transitive dependency closure for Go substrate
description:
Split from 0nz. The 'what depends on X?' half — complements the existing arch_dependencies (which answers 'what does X depend on?').

Tool spec:
- Name: arch_blast_radius
- Method: ArchBlastRadius
- Category: analysis
- Args: target file or package path (`target string`), plus existing Path/Repo/ScanControl pattern
- Returns: list of nodes whose transitive dependency closure includes target, with the dependency chain that reaches it
- Use case: 'if I change file X, what else needs review?'

Implementation notes:
- Reuse existing graph in ArchGraph.ResolvedEdges() to walk reverse edges
- Add reverse-edge index helper if needed (check internal/model first)
- Default depth cap to prevent runaway on circular dependencies (suggest 50; existing arch_dataflow uses 20)
- Tool description per existing 'USE WHEN / FAILS WHEN' pattern in tools/definitions.go

Acceptance:
1. arch_blast_radius implemented for Go substrate
2. ToolSpec quality reviewed (USE WHEN / FAILS WHEN format)
3. golangci-lint clean, gosec/govulncheck via CI
4. Test coverage for: simple transitive closure, depth cap, missing target node, circular deps

Parent: code-to-arch-mcp-0nz (split for scope)
Reference: braedonsaunders/codeflow design demonstrates substrate-agnostic graph algorithm
close_reason: arch_blast_radius shipped in PR #6 / 291cd52. Pure BFS algorithm in detector/blast_radius.go, handler in tools/handlers.go, 7 test cases including cycle safety and depth cap. Tool count 17 → 18.

title: Adoption trial: zilliztech/claude-context as MCP-installed semantic search
description:
Spun off from ycx after Thread B (Merkle DAG comparison doc) shipped in PR #4 / commit c6f467e.

Thread A from original ycx, kept intact:

1. Install via `claude mcp add claude-context -- npx @zilliz/claude-context-mcp@latest` with Zilliz Cloud + OpenAI embeddings
2. Index 1-2 real codebases (candidates: core-bl for lmwtfy work, or public360-mcp-server itself)
3. Use for a week across actual tasks
4. Note: retrieval quality, latency, cost (Zilliz + OpenAI embeddings), failure modes
5. Decide: keep / adopt / drop

Source: https://github.com/zilliztech/claude-context
Background: 23-04-2026 sift entry on Anthropic 'Building Agents That Reach Production Systems With MCP'.

Note: this is a multi-week trial, not a single-session task. Keep open as long as the trial is in progress.
close_reason: Won't-do for now. Multi-week adoption trial cost (Zilliz Cloud + OpenAI embeddings spend, indexing 1-2 real codebases, week of actual use) is high relative to the value, given the portfolio is already dense. Re-open if priorities shift toward semantic code search as a daily tool. Original Thread A spec preserved in the bead description for future reference.

title: Add arch_blast_radius tool + extend arch_scan to markdown substrate
description: Two related tool additions inspired by braedonsaunders/codeflow (https://github.com/braedonsaunders/codeflow, 2.5K stars, last push 26-04-2026) sift'd 26-04-2026. **Tool 1: arch_blast_radius**. Takes a file path and returns the transitive dependency closure (what depends on X). Complements existing arch_dependencies (what X depends on). Use case: Claude Code asks 'if I change file X what else needs review?' Currently no tool answers this directly. **Tool 2: extend arch_scan to markdown substrate**. Currently arch_scan parses Go imports. Extend to handle Obsidian vault / markdown directory as input. Edges: wiki-links `[[note]]` and relative markdown links `[text](./relative.md)`. Output: same node/edge shape as Go scan. Use case: 'scan my AI-Knowledge directory and tell me which notes are orphans / hubs / highly-cross-linked.' Already partially covered by knowledge-lint skill but knowledge-lint doesn't produce a graph artifact. **Strategic move**: codeflow demonstrates substrate-agnostic graph algorithm (one tool, multiple substrates: code + obsidian + future). Worth considering as v2 architecture for code-to-arch-mcp: substrate-detection layer + graph algorithm layer. Reading log: 26-04-2026 Great-rated entry. **Acceptance**: (1) arch_blast_radius implemented for Go substrate, (2) arch_scan extended to .md substrate with wiki-link edge detection, (3) integration test against own AI-Knowledge directory (~150 notes, expect to find orphans/hubs), (4) tool descriptions updated per existing definitions.go conventions, (5) golangci-lint clean, gosec clean, govulncheck clean, (6) ToolSpec quality reviewed by go-mcp-reviewer agent.
close_reason: Split into two child beads: arch_blast_radius (transitive dep closure) and markdown substrate extension. Both retain the original acceptance criteria and reference. Parent purpose (group both ideas) achieved through children.

title: Add clean-clone CI job to catch WIP-dependent main commits
description:
Add a CI job that builds and tests from a fresh clone (not a cached workspace).

Discovered during 22-04-2026 SDK bump sweep: main HEAD was found to not compile standalone. Commit `e346a99` added calls to `safepath.ValidateOutputPath` in `tools/handlers.go:631, 882` but the function definition only existed in uncommitted local WIP. The bug went undetected because every local checkout had the WIP.

Fixed retroactively in commit `b8fbdf9` (safepath: add ValidateOutputPath for not-yet-existing file paths).

Fix to prevent recurrence:
1. Add a `clean-clone` job to CI that does `git clone`, `go build ./...`, `go test ./...` from a pristine checkout on every push
2. Ensure this job runs parallel to (not cached alongside) other CI jobs
3. Job must fail if main cannot stand on its own without local modifications

This is a belt-and-suspenders for solo-maintained repos where every dev workspace tends to have local WIP.
close_reason: Clean-clone CI job merged in PR #3 (squash 92cd3d8)
